---
title: "1.9 Method Signatures"
subtitle: "Part 1: Method Basics and Flow of Control"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Methods and procedural abstraction

Up until now, all of our code has been in the `main` method, but complex programs are made up of many methods.

When faced with a large problem, we can break it down into smaller subproblems and solve each subproblem separately.

Each of these subproblems can be a method.

## Method

### Named block of code

A <span class="term">method</span> (方法) is a named block of code that performs a task when it is called.

A <span class="term">block</span> of code is any section of code enclosed in curly braces like `{ }`.

These named blocks of code have many names in different programming languages: functions, procedures, subroutines, etc.

In Java they are called methods.

## Procedural Abstraction

### Use without knowing every detail

<span class="term">Procedural abstraction</span> (过程抽象) allows a programmer to use a method and not worry about the details of how it exactly works.

For example, we do not need to know how a car exactly works in order to drive.

We know that if we hit the brakes, the car will stop.

Similarly, we can use a method someone else wrote without knowing exactly how it works.

## Why Methods

### Organize and avoid repetition

We divide up a program into multiple methods in order to organize the code, reduce its complexity, and avoid repetition of code.

The song "Old MacDonald Had a Farm" has a lot of repetition.

Instead of writing the same code over and over in order to print the song, we can write methods that contain the repeated code and run them when needed.

## Repeated Code Check

### `clickablearea:: repeatedcode-old-mcdonald`

Click on all the lines that are completely identical and repeated.

```java
public static void main(String args[]) {
    System.out.println("Old MacDonald had a farm.");
    System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");
    System.out.println("And on this farm, they had a cow.");
    System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");
    System.out.println("With a moo moo here and a moo moo there");
    System.out.println("Here a moo, there a moo, everywhere a moo moo");
    System.out.println("Old MacDonald had a farm");
    System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");
}
```

## Repeated Code Answer

### Identical repeated lines

The repeated lines are:

::: {.tight-list}
- `System.out.println("Old MacDonald had a farm.");`
- `System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");`
- `System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");`
- `System.out.println("Old MacDonald had a farm");`
- `System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");`
:::

When you see repeated code, that is a signal for you to make a new method.

## Method Call

### Name plus parentheses

A <span class="term">method call</span> is when the code calls out the method's name in order to run the code in the method.

The method call always includes the method name and parentheses `()`.

Sometimes there is data inside the parentheses if the method needs that to do its job.

```java
// Method call
methodName();
```

## Method Call Example

### `println()`

When we write:

```java
System.out.println("Hello World");
```

we are calling the `println()` method to print out `"Hello World"`.

Method calls are how Java runs code stored inside a named block.

## Flow of Control

### Method calls interrupt sequential execution

Execution in Java always begins in the `main` method in the current class.

Then, the <span class="term">flow of control</span> (控制流) skips from method to method as they are called.

When a method ends, it returns to the line right after the method call.

## Old MacDonald with Methods

### `codelens:: songvizOldMcdonald`

```java
public class OldMacDonaldSong
{
  public static void intro()
  {
    System.out.println("Old MacDonald had a farm");
    chorus();
  }

  public static void chorus()
  {
    System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");
  }

  public static void main(String[] args)
  {
    intro();
    System.out.println("And on that farm they had a cow.");
    chorus();
    System.out.println("With a moo moo here and a moo moo there");
    System.out.println("Here a moo, there a moo, everywhere a moo moo");
    intro();
  }
}
```

## Flow Trace

### What happens in the song code

The `main()` method calls the `intro()` method, which calls the `chorus()` method.

When `chorus()` is called, flow skips to the `chorus` code, executes and prints `"E-I-E-I-O"`, and then returns back to the method that called it.

The methods can be in any order, but programmers usually put the `main` method at the end after all the methods that it uses are defined.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: song-add-calls`

Scroll down to the `main` method below and add lines of code to the `main` method for the second verse of the Old MacDonald Song.

Call the `intro()` and `chorus()` methods and print out lines about a duck or another animal.

## Starter Code

### `song-add-calls`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class OldMacDonaldSong
{
    public static void intro()
    {
        System.out.println("Old MacDonald had a farm");
        chorus();
    }

    public static void chorus()
    {
        System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        intro();
        System.out.println("And on that farm they had a cow.");
        chorus();
        System.out.println("With a moo moo here and a moo moo there");
        System.out.println("Here a moo, there a moo, everywhere a moo moo");

        // TODO:
        // 1. Call the method intro()

        // 2. Print out the line beginning "And on that farm"
        //    with a duck or another animal

        // 3. Call the method chorus

        // 4. Print out the lines with the appropriate animal sounds

        // 5. Call the method intro again

    }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `song-add-calls`

Runestone checks:

::: {.tight-list}
- calls to `intro()` are `3 or more`
- calls to `chorus()` are `3 or more`
:::

The new verse must call the existing methods rather than duplicating the repeated method bodies.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: songMethodsTrace`

What does the following code print out?

::: {.code-scroll .compact}
```java
public class Song
{
    public static void print()
    {
        System.out.print("I like to ");
        eat();
        eat();
        eat();
        fruit();
    }

    public static void fruit()
    {
        System.out.println("apples and bananas!");
    }

    public static void eat()
    {
        System.out.print("eat ");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        print();
    }
}
```
:::

## Quick Check Options

### `songMethodsTrace`

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `I like to eat eat eat.`
- B. `I like to eat eat eat fruit.`
- C. `I like to apples and bananas eat.`
- D. `I like to eat eat eat apples and bananas!`
- E. Nothing, it does not compile.
:::

## Answer Reasoning

### `songMethodsTrace`

Correct answer: <span class="mark">D. `I like to eat eat eat apples and bananas!`</span>

The `print` method calls `eat` three times and then calls `fruit`.

Figuring out what the code does is called <span class="term">tracing</span> through the code.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define a method as a named block of code that runs when called
- explain procedural abstraction as using a method without knowing every implementation detail
- identify repeated code as a signal for creating methods
- trace flow of control from `main()` to called methods and back
- add a second Old MacDonald verse by calling `intro()` and `chorus()`
- trace the output of `print()`, `eat()`, and `fruit()`
:::

## End

### Continue to method signatures

The next deck covers method headers, signatures, parameters, and arguments.
